alert_title: payment-service /charge p99 latency above SLO, intermittent 500s

timeline.event: Deploy: payment-service v5.3.0 (adds structured logging for charge events; changelog states no logic changes)
Deploy: auth-service v2.9.0 (adds a fraud-score lookup call inside validate_token, runs on every request)
Alert fired: payment-service /charge p99 latency above SLO

logs_excerpt: payment-service: WARN auth-service token validation call took 1840ms (baseline ~90ms)
auth-service: INFO validate_token now calls fraud-score-service as of v2.9.0 deploy
payment-service: WARN worker pool utilization at 88%, queue depth rising
postgres: INFO query latency nominal, no entries in slow query log
redis: INFO cache hit rate 95%, eviction rate nominal

candidate_causes.label: auth-service v2.9.0 added a synchronous fraud-score lookup to every token validation call, increasing auth-service's own latency ~20x, which made payment-service's calls to auth-service block longer and exhaust its worker pool
candidate_causes.mechanism: The new fraud-score-service call inside validate_token added a blocking dependency to every auth check; auth-service's own p99 jumped from a 90ms baseline to 1840ms, and because payment-service holds a worker thread for the duration of each auth-service call, the slowdown directly consumed payment-service's pool capacity even though payment-service's own code never changed.

candidate_causes.label: payment-service's own v5.3.0 deploy introduced a performance regression in the charge flow
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's a deploy to the symptomatic service itself, shipped not long before the alert -- the classic proximate-cause trap.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the changelog stating the deploy only added structured logging with no logic changes, and payment_service_cpu_pct staying at a normal 42%.

candidate_causes.label: Database or cache degradation is slowing down payment-service
candidate_causes.why_tempting: DB/cache issues are the most common real-world cause of payment-path latency, and payment-service's pool is visibly near capacity.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by postgres_query_latency_p50_ms at a normal 12ms and redis_cache_hit_rate_pct at a healthy 95% with nominal eviction.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Make the fraud-score-service lookup in auth-service's validate_token path asynchronous or cached (or roll back/feature-flag the v2.9.0 fraud-lookup change) -- do not roll back payment-service v5.3.0, which is unrelated.
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 2: single clean red herring (payment-service's own deploy, ruled out by its own changelog + normal CPU) plus a classic-but-wrong DB/cache guess (ruled out by two nominal metrics). True cause is one hop upstream in auth-service, requiring the responder to trace payment-service's pool exhaustion back through its dependency on auth-service rather than blaming the symptomatic service's own recent deploy by proximity.

alert_title: cart-service checkout error rate above 5%, intermittent timeouts on add-to-cart

timeline.event: Deploy: cart-service v4.2.0 (UI copy/translation changes only; changelog states no backend logic modified)
Third-party identity provider OktaCorp reports elevated latency on its token-introspection endpoint (public status page)
auth-service p99 latency on OktaCorp introspection calls climbs from an 80ms baseline to 2200ms
payment-service request queue depth rises while calling auth-service for payment-token validation
Alert fired: cart-service checkout error rate above 5%

logs_excerpt: cart-service: WARN payment pre-check call to payment-service timed out after 3000ms (32 occurrences in 5m)
payment-service: WARN auth-service token validation call exceeded 2000ms (58 occurrences in 5m)
auth-service: WARN OktaCorp introspection endpoint p99 at 2200ms (baseline 80ms)
cart-service: INFO redis-cache GET for cart-session averaging 4ms (nominal)
cart-service: ERROR add-to-cart request failed after retry exhaustion

candidate_causes.label: OktaCorp's (third-party identity provider) token-introspection endpoint degraded, increasing auth-service's validation latency, which queued payment-service's calls to auth-service and exhausted its worker pool, cascading into cart-service checkout timeouts two hops upstream
candidate_causes.mechanism: OktaCorp's own status page and auth-service's logs both show introspection latency jumping from 80ms to 2200ms while auth-service's own CPU stays normal at 35%, meaning the slowdown is external, not local. Because payment-service blocks a worker thread per auth-service call, that latency directly consumed payment-service's pool (91% utilization), and because cart-service's checkout flow makes a synchronous payment pre-check call to payment-service, the exhaustion propagated two hops upstream into cart-service's own timeouts -- without any code or infrastructure change inside cart-service itself.

candidate_causes.label: cart-service's v4.2.0 deploy introduced a regression in the checkout flow
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's the most recent deploy to the symptomatic service itself -- the proximate-cause trap.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the changelog stating the deploy is UI copy/translation only with no backend logic modified, and cart_service_cpu_pct remaining a normal 38%.

candidate_causes.label: cart-service's Redis cache is degraded, slowing down checkout
candidate_causes.why_tempting: Redis is a direct, visible dependency of cart-service and a classic latency culprit, and the incident is cache-adjacent on its face.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by cart_service_redis_hit_rate_pct at a healthy 96% and GET latency at a nominal 4ms.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Add a timeout + circuit breaker (with a fail-open or cached fallback) around auth-service's calls to OktaCorp's introspection endpoint so a third-party degradation can't block payment-service and cart-service; do not roll back cart-service v4.2.0 (unrelated) or scale cart-service's Redis (already healthy).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 3: true cause is two hops upstream (cart-service -> payment-service -> auth-service -> OktaCorp), and the two candidates visible without tracing the dependency chain are both wrong -- cart-service's own deploy (proximate-cause trap) and cart-service's own Redis (direct-dependency trap, the thing actually labeled 'cache' in this incident). Tests whether the system reasons through payment-service as an intermediate hop rather than stopping at the first plausible local-looking explanation.
